data.title: SS&C Technologies: Greater Appeal, Greater Doubts
data.description:
In late summer I concluded that SS&C Technologies (SSNC) was seeing a steady performance amidst continued M&A efforts. The hedge fund & private equity administrator enjoyed solid momentum in its share price, and while overall valuations were still very reasonable, I had some reservations given the positioning and leverage taken on.

Shares have recently come down quite a bit, creating an opportune time to revisit the investment thesis here. Continued growth, modest margin gains, a solid guidance for 2026, and shares falling further make it clear that expectations have come down. While this is appealing, and I am appealed to buy the pullback, the rise of AI makes me cautious as well.

This makes it so that appeal is on the rise, but conviction to buy the dip is down a bit at the same time, a conclusion that applies to a greater part of the software complex.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Record 2025 Results

Early in February, SS&C reported solid 2025 results with full-year sales up nearly 7% to $6.27 billion, as reported growth rates topped 8% in the final quarter. Already fat adjusted EBITDA margins near 40% were up another 40 basis points on the year before, with EBITDA reported at $2.46 billion, and adjusted earnings up 13% to $6.14 per share.

GAAP earnings only came in at $3.15 per share, basically half the adjusted earnings. The vast majority of the reconciliation stems from amortization charges, yet a $258 million stock-based compensation expense is not an adjustment that I am willing to make. That is equal to about a dollar per share on a pre-tax basis, and if I adjust for that, realistic earnings are seen near $5.50 per share.

Net debt is reported at $7.01 billion by year-end, for a leverage ratio of just over 2.8 times. That leverage ratio increased a bit during the year, with over a billion being spent on M&A during the year. That related (largely) to a GBP 766 million deal to acquire Calastone, a global funds network and technology solution provider.

While leverage is a bit high, expectations have come in quite a bit, as a stock trading in the $80s last summer traded at $90 as the year ended, with shares now down to just $68 per share. Based on realistic earnings around $5.50 per share by my math above, equity trades at just 12-13 times earnings.

More Growth, Still Doubts

For the fiscal year 2026, SS&C guides for sales to advance to a range between $6.65 and $6.81 billion, which is up around 7-8% compared to 2025. Adjusted earnings are seen between $6.70-$7.02 per share, up around 11-12% at the midpoint of the guidance, as this is comfortable in relation to the valuation and leverage coming down.

The 251 million shares have now fallen to the $68 mark, for a $17 billion equity valuation and a roughly $24 billion enterprise valuation. So despite the growth, manageable leverage, and a low realistic earnings multiple, the market seems quite skeptical of the business here.

Clearly, investors are somewhat scared that the business might not be as sustainable going forward. While AI of course, could help the business in performing services in a more efficient way, investors clearly fear that it could make SS&C's services less useful, or create less demand as well.

After all, SS&C provides services like front-and back-office, mutual fund transfer agency, regulatory reporting, performance analytics, and risk analytics. These are definitely services that could (partially) be performed by AI, as that could have real implications for the 29,000 workers employed by the business. In fact, the company has been introducing AI agents to its lineup of offerings on its own.

What Now?

Having historically held a tiny position in the shares, which, unfortunately, I did not sell out of last year, I find myself performing a balancing act. Leverage is manageable, growth is decent, the outlook is solid, realistic earnings are fine as the situation looks cheap and appealing. Low expectations are driven by the AI scare, which is absolutely interpreted by the market as a scare or potential disruptor instead of an opportunity here.

This leaves investors quite in the dark, as the past allocation of funds was the right one, with some $12 billion spent on M&A since 2018.

The debate about whether current levels represent a great opportunity or AI provides a secular threat resulted in a heated discussion on a recent conference call. On that call, CEO Bill Stone got into an interesting debate with an analyst about the impact of AI on the pricing of the firm. Mr. Stone argued that analysts should go after technology firms or their own (hedge fund) financial communities. He claimed that the 5-6 basis points charged by SS&C were not going to make the difference, as people and clients pay for service, not necessarily hours spent.

To be fair, the rise of AI has made me a bit more cautious on the shares than I was in the summer, even if it is not showing up in the pace of growth here. This growth is still solid, as the same applies to margins and earnings. Given all this, I am naturally a bit cautious, yet with shares down further, appeal is seen by that logic in isolation. All this makes me more upbeat here from a valuation perspective, and while my concerns about the business and model are on the rise, I am turning a bit more cautious.

Hence, I might be buying more shares on the dip, but I need a bigger dip to add to a tiny position here, as I fear the risks. If such risks are overstated and/or might even provide a tailwind, real opportunities can be found here, but that applies to the wider software space as well.

data.title: DIVO: Safe Dividend ETF For Retirees
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the Amplify CWP Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (DIVO), I discussed how the fund outperformed higher yielding peers. DIVO has a strong portfolio approach that provides defensive positioning and a high yield as a result of the option writing strategy. I issued a buy rating for my last coverage and since then the market has pulled back from its highs. Despite this, DIVO has outperformed the S&P 500 Index (SPX) and provided positive total returns for investors.

However, something more specific brought me back to revisit DIVO. I have several family members that have crossed into the retirement phase of their lives and now they are trying to structure their portfolio in a more defensive nature that also provides a sizeable yield. Instead of chasing growth companies, retirees may prefer to allocate capital to the steady blue-chip companies that have a long established history of business success with multiple streams of revenue. After assessing DIVO's strategy, I believe that DIVO is a very efficient ETF for investors now crossed into retirement for the following reasons:

  • Defensive Positioning
  • High Dividend Yield
  • Tax-Efficiency
  • Sector Diversity
  • Growth That Outpaces Inflation
  • Preserves Capital

When looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that DIVO's share price has increased by more than 23%. The fund continues to demonstrate its ability to participate in the positive market momentum. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to nearly 31.5% over the same time frame. DIVO now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 6.3% and issues those payouts on a monthly basis.

Fund Strategy Provides Income Security

According to the latest fund overview, DIVO now has total net assets of $6.7B that are spread across 32 different holdings. DIVO takes a very focused approach with its strategy and doesn't aim to replicate any specific index. Additionally, the fund is actively managed so it has a relatively high expense ratio of 0.56% of managed assets. However, this higher expense ratio may be justified considering the high yield that is offered to investors. The active management also extends to DIVO's option writing strategy.

In order to generate the amplified income levels needed to support the high dividend yield of 6.3%, the fund will write call options on a small portion of its individual holdings. Management tends to focus on OTM (out-of-the-money) options so that some upside growth can be captured from its holdings. DIVO will usually write options with a strike price that is at least 5% above the current market price of a position, which means that the fund is capable of collecting option premiums and seeing capital appreciation.

The frequency in which management writes options is really dependent on market conditions, but the typical range sits somewhere between 20%-30% of the portfolio. This means that during strong market rallies, up to 80% of DIVO's portfolio can grow uncapped alongside the positive market momentum. Conversely, a greater frequency of options may be written during sideways or declining markets, which allows the option premiums to serve as a buffer against losses.

One of the reasons that DIVO is great for retirees is the potential for capital appreciation despite all of the income strategies running in the background. A lot of retirees tend to lean towards sectors like utilities or energy, which provides stable but slow growth. DIVO still provides a meaningful allocation to higher growth sectors. For instance, DIVO has a 23.43% allocation to financial investments and a 13.95% allocation to technology companies.

Instead of exposing investors to many of the high growth, high beta companies in the indices, the portfolio takes a more strategic approach with its positioning. The holdings are made up of companies that have a strong history of dividend and earnings growth over time. Management specifically screens for companies that have a positive return on equity, healthy free cash flows, and avoids yield traps.

So when looking at the holdings, we can see that DIVO provides diversified exposure to some of the highest quality businesses in the world. For instance, the top holding is RTX Corp (RTX) at 5.46% of assets. This is followed by other notable companies, including Caterpillar, Inc. (CAT), The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Apple Inc. (AAPL), and The Home Depot, Inc. (HD), just to name a few. Since the fund focuses on companies with an established history of raising dividends, you will not find non-dividend payers like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) or Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) as part of the holdings.

Dividend Utility & Tax Efficiency

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1788 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 6.3%. The high yield makes it possible for retirees to live off distributions and never have to sell their shares. Looking back on the fund's dividend history over the last few years, DIVO has maintained a stellar track record of consistent payouts. However, the fund is a bit different from more traditional dividend growth ETFs. Since a portion of the distributions are determined by the success of the underlying option writing strategy, the actual distributions may slightly vary over time. However, the history below clarifies that these small payout adjustments tend to be very small.

You may have noticed that the last payout for December 2025 was extraordinarily high compared to its typical payout range. Since the fund's option strategy can harness periods of volatility and turn it into income, DIVO may end up generating a lot more income than it anticipates. Therefore, it is totally possible for DIVO to issue large supplemental distributions at the end of the year. While there isn't a guarantee that DIVO will always issue a supplemental distribution, it is a nice way for patient investors to get rewarded through periods of market volatility.

Since the fund offers a high starting dividend yield, I would not anticipate any sort of traditional dividend growth over time. However, this doesn't mean that DIVO isn't a great option for retirees. Since the fund has maintained a track record of consistent payouts, investors can essentially create their own dividend growth over time by reinvesting distributions or adding new capital over time.

To demonstrate this concept, I ran a back test of an initial $100,000 investment at the start of 2017. This back test assumes that no additional capital was ever invested, but it does assume that all dividends received were reinvested back into the accumulation of more shares. By simply remaining invested and staying patient, investors would have seen their annual dividend income more than triple. So for investors that may be approaching the retirement age within the next five years, DIVO is a very suitable position. Here are the results of the back test:

2017 Dividend Income: $4,531

2025 Dividend Income: $18,180

Even though the fund uses options to amplify its income levels, the distributions received from DIVO are still quite tax-efficient. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 65% of the distributions paid YTD were classified as return of capital. Return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. If you're a retiree, it's like that you have no intentions of selling so this works to your benefit.

However, tax-efficient doesn't necessarily equate to tax-free. The tax burden of DIVO really comes down to your specific position size, income tax bracket, and other factors so I would consult with a tax professional. The remaining 35% of distributions paid were classified as net investment income, which do have the highest tax burden for investors. So if you want to avoid this headache altogether, it may make the most sense to utilize DIVO within a tax-advantaged account.

Defensive Positioning

DIVO is great for retirees because the underlying focus on high quality dividend companies tends to translate to capital preservation through market declines. With rising geopolitical risk related to Iran, rising unemployment, and higher interest rates, market indices have continued to trend sideways. Furthermore, the market continues to selloff of software companies continues. The rising threat of AI has investors questioning the moat of software companies, so the technology sector has had a rough start to 2026.

While DIVO does provide exposure to technology holdings, management's strategy of focusing on diversified businesses helps offset these concerns. On a YTD basis, DIVO's total return sits around 4.1%, which outpaces the standard index-tracking funds. For instance, the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) is down about 0.6%, while the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) is still down by more than 1.2%. If the markets continue to trend sideways over the next twelve months, I anticipate that DIVO's outperformance is likely to continue. The combination of the high quality positions and option income an serve as a buffer to market uncertainty.

The efficiency of DIVO's strategy has also proved to be effective through prolonged market declines. For instance, the Fed aggressively hiked interest rates through 2022 in an effort to combat the uncertainty of the pandemic and inflation at the time. SPY saw a total decline of 18.17% and QQQ experienced a more severe loss of 32.58% through 2022. However, DIVO investors were much happier since the fund preserved capital and only experienced a much more tolerable loss of 1.08% over the same time frame.

Risks

Although DIVO has proven to be a solid defensive position, there are some tradeoffs that investors must consider. By protecting capital, DIVO has to exclude some growth companies to reduce volatility. So when markets do rally, DIVO's portfolio positioning and included option writing will limit the fund's ability to fully participate in the positive momentum. For instance, DIVO does underperform SPY and QQQ over the last decade. DIVO excludes companies that don't have an established track record of meaningful dividend growth. So although companies like Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) all pay a dividend, they are excluded from DIVO.

Similarly, DIVO takes a more focused approach to its holdings. Since the portfolio has less than 40 total holdings, the fund does expose investors to concentration risk to some degree. For instance, DIVO's top ten holdings make up over ~49% of total assets, with a few of its top holdings having a weight of more than 5%. So if there is ever some sort of black swan event that takes equities down across sectors, DIVO's ability to preserve capital may not be as efficient as it was in the past. Since DIVO doesn't track an underlying index, investors are the mercy of management's ability to rotate in and out of underperformers. So if management misreads market events, the fund's performance may suffer.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that DIVO is a great option for retirees because of its income generation, defensive positioning, and ability to provide attractive total returns. The fund takes an actively managed approach to its portfolio by focusing on high quality dividend companies. If the indices continue to trade choppy through 2026, I believe that outperformance may continue. DIVO's included option strategy may be able to generate heightened option premiums, which can translate to a large year-end supplemental. Furthermore, the majority of the distributions remain tax-efficient and this can be great for investors looking to minimize the tax impact from other high yield assets.

data.title: STIP ETF: Moderate But All-Around Return
data.description:
The iShares 0-5 Year TIPS Bond ETF (STIP) delivered a 3% return since my previous coverage, being a good instrument to hedge the recent market volatility. Last time I wrote that TIPS could be more attractive instruments than treasuries, as the current setup seems to offer an asymmetric opportunity to profit from a higher-than-expected inflation outlook and the monetary policy easing. Indeed, the geopolitical tension in the Middle East has sharpened the inflation outlook due to the spike in the global energy prices. Although Fed officials indicated that the central bank would not raise rates in response to oil shocks, the future monetary policy path remains uncertain. Furthermore, the market participants significantly reassessed their interest rate expectations, suggesting no actions from the policymakers at least until the second half of 2027. Still, I believe that inflation-linked government bonds are more attractive instruments in the current macroeconomic environment. TIPS could benefit from any monetary policy and inflation scenario, providing a moderate all-around return. Thus, I will keep my Hold rating on the STIP ETF with expectations for a 6% to 8% return over the next 12-month investment horizon.

Fund overview

The iShares 0-5 Year TIPS Bond ETF provides exposure to a portfolio of short-term inflation-protected U.S. Treasury bonds with remaining maturities of less than five years. The fund is composed of government bonds whose face value rises with inflation and protects investors from general price appreciation. The inflation linkers are attractive instruments to hedge the real value of the investments while providing a moderate return. In particular, the fund delivered a 3% total return since my previous coverage, while at the same time, the annual inflation rate in the U.S. stood at 2.4% in February 2026, which is the lowest reading since May last year.

Last time I wrote that the SPIT ETF allows investors to profit from inflation indexation as well as from the monetary policy easing. The inflation fears used to be linked to the Trump administration's tariff considerations. But the Iran war and disruption around the Strait of Hormuz took their toll on the global energy markets, sending the crude oil prices above $100 a barrel. And while there is still no clear evidence about the impact of tariffs on the wider economy, the Middle East conflict merely adds up to the inflationary pressure.

As a result, the Federal Reserve made a decision to hold its benchmark interest rate in the 3.50%/3.75% range, making it the third consecutive meeting without a rate change. At the same time, the market participants significantly reassessed their expectations regarding the Fed's interest rate monetary policy.

The current Fed futures suggest that the first rate cut would take place in September next year, which underscores the growing expectations that interest rates may remain elevated for longer than previously anticipated. It’s possible that inflationary effects could be more persistent in the near term, which could take policymakers some time to assess the complex and uncertain factors. In addition, the market environment is already saturated with heightened risk, which is another reason why the Federal Reserve could maintain its monetary policy stance for a longer time frame.

And despite the increased market volatility, the fund remains an attractive investment vehicle with a decent dividend policy. In particular, STIP boasts a 3.4% dividend yield on a 12-month basis, which is prominently higher compared to the 2.6% yield of the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLU).

Looking at the performance, the fund slightly outperforms the Vanguard Short-Term Inflation-Protected Securities ETF (VTIP) since the beginning of the year. The fund is an attractive instrument for hedging the market volatility as well, especially when comparing it to the broad market.

Investment conclusion

Let’s now take into account the interest rates and inflation developments to evaluate the fund’s future prospects. STIP ETF has an effective duration of 2.36 years, which remains close to two-year maturity government bonds. So let’s take a look at the 2-year Treasury rate dynamics.

The 2-year treasury yield popped up significantly since the beginning of the year to 3.81%. This increase could be explained by the geopolitical developments, which add a new layer to the macroeconomic uncertainty. The renewed inflationary pressure indicates higher-for-longer interest rates, though the Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell suggested the central bank would not raise rates in response to oil shocks. Note that we are talking about the nominal yield, which is a function of the real TIPS yield and the break-even inflation. And given the fact that the fund contains primarily 5-year and 10-year TIPS, its real return could be around 1.6%. Hence, the market-implied break-even inflation could be estimated at circa 2.2%, and we can clearly see this on the following chart.

In case inflation ends up being higher than that, TIPS would be a more attractive instrument than Treasuries. In fact, the short-term inflation expectations increased with the surging energy prices. According to the latest surveys, inflation is now seen at 3.4%, up from 3% last month. As a result, I believe that TIPS are no longer just an inflation protection but a viable instrument for moderate real returns.

So, let’s move to the point. What if interest rates remain elevated for a longer time? Or do policymakers decide to implement rate cuts this year? Or do geopolitical tensions in the Middle East intensify the inflation rate?

These three questions are the reason I remain optimistic about the STIP ETF. In my view, the fund is a good instrument to hold in case either of the above three scenarios takes place.

In particular, the weighted average coupon rate of the underlying bonds in the fund amounts to 1.03, which means a 1% coupon yield. If the Fed officials decide to keep interest rates unchanged for a longer time, the fund would benefit its investors with a decent dividend yield of around 3.5%. The difference between the coupon yield and the dividend yield of the fund comes from the TIPS’ built-in feature of principal adjustments by the ratio of the Consumer Price Index, meaning that the ETF pays out the inflation adjustment as an income to investors. And given that the near-term inflation outlook ranges from 2.3% to 3.4%, I believe that a figure of around 3% could prevail over the following 12-month horizon. Hence, the nominal value of the bonds could adjust accordingly, bringing potentially up to a 3% NAV gain.

In addition, the fund has four bonds maturing in the following 12-month period, and with the addition of the new TIPS instruments to the portfolio, the duration could approach 3 years. Moreover, the target duration of the fund could increase up to 5 years, which would be highly regarded in the scenario of interest rate cuts, providing an additional return.

And to sum up, I believe that it’s reasonable to expect 6% to 8% return from the STIP ETF over the next 12-month horizon. In my view, the fund could provide a moderate, but all-around, investment return in either monetary policy or inflation scenario. For that reason, I will keep my Hold ration on the STIP ETF, since TIPS instruments are more appealing compared to treasuries in my view.

data.title: MarineMax: These Waters Aren't Smooth Enough For My Liking
data.description:
Back in late December of last year, I decided to downgrade shares of MarineMax (HZO) from a "Buy" to a "Hold." I did not make this decision lightly. However, fundamental performance for the business was weakening, and I was becoming increasingly concerned about the state of the economy. The high net debt that the business has also didn't help here. Since then, however, things have gone better than expected. Rising revenue has resulted in pushing shares of the business up 9.2% at a time when the S&P 500 is down 6.3%. Management is forecasting a meaningful improvement in profitability this year. And, at least in some respects, it's not like the stock is particularly pricey. Having said that, I do think that there are still legitimate reasons to be worried. Although management might have created some optimism through the announcement of a new share buyback program, the amount of debt that the business has is still concerning. And when you add on top of this my own view that the economy is almost certain to experience a recession in the next 12 months, remaining cautious with a "Hold" rating is the obvious choice.

Taking Another Spin on MarineMax

At this time, the only new data that investors have access to regarding MarineMax covers through the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. But before we get into that, I do think that a brief discussion of what the business is and what it does is warranted. For those new to the company, it operates as the world's largest recreational boat and yacht retailer, marina operator, and superyacht services company. It has over 70 retail locations spread across 21 states, from which it sells both new and used recreational boats and marine-related products. For those interested in this space, that is certainly attractive. And when you layer on top of this other operations that the company has, such as its ownership of Fraser Yachts Group, a superyacht brokerage and luxury yacht services company; Northrop & Johnson, another similar enterprise; and SkipperBud’s, one of the largest boat sales, brokerage, service, and marina and storage groups in the United States, not to mention other assets that include the production of sport yachts, day boats, and the like, we have a real ecosystem dedicated to the marine recreation market.

When it comes to the first quarter of 2026, the picture for the business was surprisingly impressive on the top line. Revenue totaled $505.2 million. That was up nicely from the $468.5 million that the company reported a year earlier. This 7.8% rise in sales was attributable in large part to a 10.7% jump in comparable store sales that more than offset the company’s closure of other locations. However, this does not mean that the overall adjusted organic picture for the business is great. The first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was actually negatively impacted by hurricanes Helene and Milton. So if anything, this is primarily a recovery following a short-term window of weakness.

Even though revenue for the company improved, the bottom line worsened. The business went from a net profit of $18.1 million to a net loss of $7.9 million. There were a couple of different factors here. As an example, the company's gross profit contracted by 5.4%, declining from 36.2% of sales to 31.8%. This was blamed by management on higher promotional activities aimed at countering what the company described as "challenging retail conditions" and an unfavorable sales mix. We also saw certain costs rise, such as selling, general, and administrative expenses, with management attributing that to changes in the fair value of contingent consideration liabilities.

Other profitability metrics for the business largely worsened as well. On an adjusted basis, net profits went from $4.1 million to negative $4.6 million. Operating cash flow did improve drastically from negative $146.1 million to positive $16.9 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $8.5 million to negative $0.6 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company plummeted from $26.1 million to $15.5 million.

Despite these issues, management did give investors one reason to be optimistic. The firm mentioned in their latest earnings call that they anticipate EBITDA to be between $110 million and $125 million this year. That's unchanged from prior guidance and reflects an eventual improvement in industry inventory levels and gross margins following retail margin pressure that should affect the company negatively through the second quarter of the year. Adjusted earnings per share, meanwhile, should come in at between $0.40 and $0.95. At the midpoint, that would mean adjusted net profits of $14.8 million compared to the $13.9 million that the company reported for 2025. Management is working hard on making certain changes as well. For instance, management applauded itself for an almost $170 million reduction in inventory year over year. That's certainly freed up cash and reduces the risk of an oversupply in the industry that could push margins even lower. A reduction in debt has also permitted the company to reduce interest expense. For context, at the end of the first quarter of 2025, net debt was $1.03 billion. That has since dropped to $921.2 million.

Based on the expectations that management has for profitability for the year, it's likely that adjusted operating cash flow will come in at around $74.9 million. That would be a slight improvement over the $70.3 million reported for last year. If we use all of these estimates, not to mention the results the company achieved in 2025, we can see in the chart below how the company is priced. These multiples aren't bad by any means. But they certainly don't put the company in the undervalued category. Even relative to other similar firms, the stock is more or less fairly valued. In that same chart, you can see that three of the four companies that I stacked it up against are cheaper than it is on both a price-to-earnings basis and on an EV-to-EBITDA basis. Only on a price-to-operating cash flow basis is the stock cheap, with it coming in below any of the institutions I compared it to.

One big issue that I have regarding the company is its overall debt position. Even though net debt has dropped as of late, it still translates to a forward net leverage ratio for the company of 7.84. If management were working on drastically reducing this further, I would feel a bit more optimistic. But on March 4, the company announced a new $100 million share buyback program to replace the one that expired that same month. There is no guarantee that the company will buy back shares. But we know that, under the old plan, the firm had repurchased 1.4 million units. Given the leverage situation that the company is faced with, paying down debt makes more sense.

There is, however, a potential catalyst that exists here. On February 3rd, management acknowledged that the company received an unsolicited indication of interest from The Donerail Group whereby that party offered to acquire all outstanding units for $35 per share. Considering that the stock is currently at $27.15 as I type this, that would translate to a pretty nice premium if a deal is arrived at. So far, there has been no agreement. However, management did mention in a press release issued on February 24 that the two parties are communicating. This does create some opportunity for upside, though it is admittedly speculative.

Takeaway

I would argue that MarineMax is most certainly an interesting prospect. But it's not one that I consider to be attractive enough to turn bullish on right now. This is despite the fact of the catalyst existing, and it can be chalked up to concerns about the economy more broadly, combined with overall leverage that the business has. For now, I think that maintaining it as a "Hold" makes the most sense.

data.title: I Am Still A Believer In REGL
data.description:
Last spring, I wrote my first analysis for the ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrat ETF (REGL). I rated the fund as a Buy not only for its strong track record of gains when compared to its peers but also the downside protection that it offers when the market is not offering rising tides to lift all boats. Since that article was published on May 15, REGL has produced a total return of 13.9%. This is about 1.5% less than the gains produced by the S&P 500 and 2.5% less than the total return of the State Street SPDR S&P MidCap 400 ETF Trust (MDY), which I used as a comparison for my previous analysis.

Justification for investing in mid-cap stocks can be presented in a number of ways, but perhaps the most compelling is the graph below. From July of 1991 through the end of 2025, the S&P MidCap 400 has outperformed the S&P 500 by 54 basis points when comparing their compound average growth rates. It has topped the Russell 2000 by an even wider margin. The question then becomes one of uncovering the most effective way to invest, based on one’s own investment goals. In the case of REGL, the focus is on mid-cap companies that have exhibited dependable earnings, strong underlying fundamentals, and track records of profitability and growth that allow them to increase their dividends to shareholders on a consistent basis.

I consider myself to be a conservative investor, which means capital preservation is more important to me than it might be for someone 20 years younger. The advantage that REGL still presents is one of growth without exceessive risk. The fund has a strong history of positive returns that is not going away anytime soon, and the next few months may be a great example of that. Already in 2026, a year in which the S&P 500 has lost almost 1% of its value, REGL has gained 7.2%, topping the more popular large-cap index by 8% but also outpacing MDY by 0.2%. I am still a believer that REGL is the right fund for investors like myself and worth buying for those who may not have exposure to the mid-cap space.

ETF Overview

The ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrat ETF is a passively managed fund that is designed to follow the performance of the S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrats Index, an index that tracks members of the S&P MidCap 400 that have raised their dividend payouts at least once a year for 15 years or more. As a group, these stocks have increased their dividends by 12.5% since the fund’s inception in 2015. Only 16.8%, or 67 of the 400 companies that comprise the broader mid-cap index meet the criteria for inclusion in REGL.

REGL has $1.68 billion in assets under management, slightly less than the $1.73 billion it held when my last analysis was written. The fund contained 67 stocks as of the last update from its fund manager at the end of February. REGL is rebalanced quarterly and updated with new stocks that meet the dividend requirement on an annual basis. When the fund is rebalanced, all constituents are equally weighted.

The top 12 holdings as for the fund are shown below. The first name on the list, Westlake Corporation (WLK) is the only one with a weight of over 2%. The last time I wrote about REGL, WLK represented the smallest holding in the fund. Westlake is a materials producer that specializes in chemicals and fabricated building products. Its stock is up 43.1% in the last 12 months.

Food and beverage retailer Casey’s General Stores, Inc. (CASY) is second on the holdings list at 1.82% of the total assets in the fund. Its stock has gained 77.46% in the last year, including a whopping 34.22% year-to-date. A trio of gas utilities — New Jersey Resources Corporation (NJR), National Fuel Gas Company (NFG), and One Gas, Inc. (OGS) — hold places three, four, and five on the list. All are benefiting from greater energy demand with AI data centers causing some disruption in the industry. The Ensign Group, Inc. (ENSG) is the only stock that was listed in the fund’s top 12 last May that is still included on this list today. REGL is well diversified as the top 10 holdings in the fund comprise only 17.65% of its total assets. The smallest holding this time around is Thor Industries, Inc. (THO) which has an allocation of 1.00%.

The sector weighting from the fund is given in the chart below. Financial stocks have the largest allocation by far at 29.21%. Utilities, a sector known for its dividend production, is next at 16.43%. Industrials and Materials also have double-digit shares of the total assets for the ETF at 14.32% and 13.91%, respectively. The only sector currently missing from the fund is the communication sector.

The fund has an expense ratio of 0.40%, which seems high to me for a passively managed fund comprised almost entirely of US equities. Then again, the dividend-oriented funds that are listed as peers on Seeking Alpha have fund costs ranging from 0.38% to 0.74%. Given that range, REGL does not seem so out of place.

The fund has a yield of 2.23% as of April 8, perhaps a little lower than one might expect from a fund of this type with a high allocation to the financial sector. Dividends are paid quarterly with the most recent payout hitting shareholder accounts on the last day of March. Over the last 10 years, the compound average growth rate for the fund’s distributions is 11.19%.

Analysis of REGL

In my last analysis, I spend a good but of time showing the advantages of the ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrat ETF over MDY. While REGL’s total returns are not always greater than that of its more inclusive mid-cap peer, it had outperformed over longer periods of time, and that is still true today. Since its inception, REGL has produced a total return of 185.7% compared to MDY’s 171.8%.

Below is a chart of REGL’s annual returns from Morningstar dating back to 2017. REGL had some disappointing years in 2019 and 2023 when the markets were rising after a year of declines. However, it is what the fund accomplished during those years of declines in 2018 and 2022 that catch my eye. The fund did not just post “above average” returns. It was at or near the top of its peers, finishing in the top percentile in 2018 and second percentile in 2022. When one considers that the fund has been able to post a decent gain in the first three months of this year while the broader market has been in a period of decline, it is clear that this outperformance is no fluke.

This supports the information presented in the graph below from ProShares. REGL is able to capture 87% of the gains when compared to the S&P MidCap 400. That, by itself does not sound too enticing. However, the fund shares even less of its losses with the same index. In other words, REGL investors are able to participate in the ups of the sector more than the downs.

A final point to consider is the valuation of the stocks held by REGL when compared to other indices. The stocks held by REGL have an average price to earnings ratio of 17.4x and price to book ratio of 2.15x. The stocks held by MDY have an average P/E of 20.93x and price to book of 2.66. As a point of comparison, the stocks held in the S&P 500 Index have a P/E over 26x and a price to book of 4.88x. My comfort level of investing during a year like this one is rising when I know the stocks I own have a lower valuation.

Risks to Consider

REGL is particularly sensitive to higher interest rates, and long-term rates have been sliding upward in 2026. The recent activity in the Middle East has caused inflation fears in more than just the oil markets. Supply chain shortages may impact a range of goods in the months ahead if there is no quick resolution to the military activity. Ten-year treasury rates has risen about 30 basis points since the war started. We began 2026 with the expectation that we would see two additional interest rate cuts from the Federal Reserve, but those are appearing to be less likely as we move on further into the year. Financials, which are the most heavily sector in REGL, are especially sensitive to interest rates, and a continued increase in long-term rates could impact business activity and pressure margins later in the year. Every stock in the fund, even those outside the financial sector, could see added pressure as interest rates rise because their dividends become less attractive when compared to other income-producing investment options.

It is important for any investor to know that REGL will not be a top performer during periods of multiple expansion when tech stocks and other growth stocks are outperforming. This is not a fund for investors who are willing to take on a greater amount of risk in order to obtain higher long-term returns. REGL’s allocation to the entire tech sector is just 1.71%. That amount is solely attributable to one company, Littelfuse, Inc. (LFUS), which has been added to the fund in the last year. Founded in 1927, LFUS is a designer and producer of electronic components including fuses, transistors, and relays. The stock is up 114.94% in the last 12 months. According to ProShares, in the last 15 years of its consecutive dividend increases, LFUS has outperformed the S&P MidCap 400 with an annualized return of 13%.

The Bottom Line

This is not a fund for everyone. Younger investors with longer time horizons are likely to be better served in more aggressive, large-cap funds. The value for me has been the ability to designate a small portion of my overall portfolio in an ETF that delivers decent gains with meaningful downside protection. I have been an owner of REGL for several years, and there are certainly some years when the fund underperformed, making me think about other options. However, during down years, the fund reminds me of one of its strengths, protecting me from significant losses during broader market pullbacks. While I am not adding to my position at the current time, I still believe the fund is worth buying for investors who are looking for additional exposure to mid-cap stocks. The current uncertainty in the market is only serving to reinforce my past investment decision.

data.title: S&P 500: A Euphoric Market With A Sobering Ceasefire And GDP Reality
data.description:
The S&P 500 has gained 2.2% following the announcement of a ceasefire. While the reaction is not completely unexpected, I do believe that the optimism seen on the stock markets is premature as the ceasefire is fragile at best. In this report, I discuss the uncertainty that remains, which could eventually increase market volatility or in the worst case a continuation of the conflict.

Oil Prices Remain High And GDP Impact Is Still Present

There is no question that the worst has been averted in terms of oil prices, GDP impacts, and potential loss of lives. However, we note that the GDP impact from oil, LNG, and fertilizers was expected to be around 100 basis points on GDP with a 130 basis point global impact. That was for a situation where the war would last around 2-4 weeks with Brent oil at $100-$120, and those 2-4 weeks were measured from mid-March. The most optimistic scenario back then was the war ending immediately with Brent oil at $80-$88 per barrel and GDP impact expected to be 54 basis points. In this scenario inflation would be 0.7 points higher, and in the 2-4 week scenario inflation would be 1.4 points higher. So, while there is a ceasefire on the table now, there is a component of GDP and inflation that will affect growth. That seems to be irreversible. Oil is now trading at $94.85, which is substantially lower than the $115.69 peak, but it is still 44% higher than pre-war pricing. There have been 5 weeks in which prices have quickly risen, and that has an economic impact.

I believe that through elasticity, that could imply a 3%-12% downside for the S&P 500 measured from the pre-war trading levels. That would imply an S&P 500 at 6,050-6,675 points with a midpoint at around 6,365 points. The bottom was around 6,346 points, so pretty close to the midpoint of the range. At the time of writing, the S&P 500 is trading at 6,779, largely ignoring the GDP impact. So, the market is actually treating the GDP impact as a back-view mirror event, while the GDP impact still has to be absorbed. That disconnect is typical for the financial markets but may provide an indication that the market is not expecting or accurately mapping any GDP impact that has yet to come. In other words, we have seen a risk premium normalization in the markets but not yet a potential GDP impact being factored into the stock market.

The Ceasefire Is Fragile And Strait of Hormuz Remains Choked

I believe that the increase in the indices and the decline in oil prices is an optimistic one driven by optimism from President Trump. JD Vance, however, called the ceasefire a fragile one and if we look at the development, it indeed seems to be fragile. We have seen Gulf countries intercepting missiles after the ceasefire. It is a violation of the ceasefire. In some way it is also understandable. In Iran, with the elimination of Khamenei on day 1 of the strikes, there has been a power vacuum and it largely seems like the command has been decentralized meaning that it may take time before all regions and branches of command require time to adjust to the ceasefire. This is the consequence of a power vacuum, but by design this can also be created to inflict maximum damage when Iran’s leadership is no longer functional.

Furthermore, Israel may not view Lebanon as a part of the ceasefire, which may result in the ceasefire not holding. Several minutes after I wrote this sentence, the news came in that Iran signaled it may withdraw over continued attacks in Lebanon. We have also seen attacks on the Iranian oil refinery in Lavan. Whether the attack came from outside, was orchestrated by the regime, or came from inside is not known, but it feeds the view that the ceasefire is fragile and, in fact, more fragile than it seemed at the time of the announcement of the ceasefire. So far, it seems this is a truce between Iran and the US, and Israel has followed with a stop on attacks on Iran, but attacks in the region are still happening, so ceasefire means nothing more and nothing less than the US not attacking, but apart from that, the region is still in chaos.

The perception is that the Strait of Hormuz is now open for business, and the reality is that it is not. A toll that needs to be paid by shipping companies is seen as a potential joint venture by President Trump, but it actually means that Iran is gaining much needed funds on a ceasefire that may not remain in place. Iranian and Israeli new agencies are now reporting that the Strait of Hormuz is closed again. Opening the Strait of Hormuz seemed to be prerequisite for a ceasefire and the Strait now seems to be closed again less than a day after a ceasefire agreement was reached. Even if the Strait were to be fully open, it would take weeks, if not more, before supply would normalize, with some supply being lost due to damage to energy infrastructure.

Opposing Views On The 10-Point Plan

Pivotal for the ceasefire agreement seems to be a 10-point plan. It is reported that the plan includes Iran maintaining leverage of the Strait of Hormuz, removal of sanctions, removal of troops in neighboring countries, and continued nuclear enrichment. That 10-point list is a stark contrast with President Trump’s post on Truth Social and posted by the White House on X claiming no nuclear enrichment.

It seems that the U.S. 15-point plan and Iran’s 10-point plan have been mixed into a last-minute ceasefire to prevent a catastrophe. However, both parties seem to have entered lists that cannot be reconciled and that adds yet another fragile element to ceasefire.

Conclusion: Ceasefire Is A Great Thing, But This One Looks Shaky

Last night I could see four of the six KC-135 aerial tankers that are stationed at Bucharest departing when I was standing on my roof. Several hours later two tankers disappeared which often happens when fighter jets and B-52 bombers are being refueled. So, it seems we were hours away from something that could shape the global economy for years to come. In that regard, I believe the ceasefire is the best thing that could happen and I understand the positive market reaction. However, the current market reaction does not price in the fact that GDP still has been lost and oil and gas supply may take weeks to normalize, and the ceasefire is already being broken left and right, with the Strait of Hormuz seemingly being closed again. So, the conditions of the ceasefire and the ceasefire itself only exist on paper, while the market has already removed the war risk element from the stock market. I believe that the market reaction is overly enthusiastic and largely ignores economic realities as well as the reality of the fragile ceasefire. As a result, I believe that in a reasonable market setting the rally will not hold, and I believe that assigning a sell rating for now is prudent, and a more bullish view is justified based on what will be agreed on in the next two weeks.

data.title: Golar LNG: Structural LNG Tightness And Strategic Optionality Support Further Upside
data.description:
Here at the Lab, we were lucky on Golar LNG's (GLNG) initiation of coverage and follow-up note ahead of the Q3 2025 results. While we had not anticipated an escalation in the Middle East, our outlook was underpinned by supportive industry tailwinds. This was driven by structural growth stemming from heightened energy security concerns, with dynamics reinforced by Russia’s export restrictions (the EU gas diversification strategy) and the series of energy shocks in recent years. From a fundamentals view, our strong buy rating was backed by de-risked long-term contracts with a robust EBITDA backlog across multiple assets.

We report that, since mid-Oct 2025, the price of Golar LNG shares has increased by more than 40% (Fig. 1).

Fig 1

Why are we still positive?

Firstly, the company has initiated a formal process to evaluate strategic alternatives to maximize shareholders' value. Golar LNG appoints Goldman Sachs as a financial advisor. These strategic options include a company sale, asset divestment, and a merger or other business combination to optimize the company's corporate structure further. This was highlighted in our initiation of coverage, where we reported that "Golar investment may be viewed as a potential exit from an infrastructure fund targeting an unlevered annual return of 8-10%. Even if we are not speculating on M&A, this is a real case." A sale to an infra player is a potential outcome, but we believe Golar growth might also be of interest to strategic players in the oil and gas business.

Secondly, considering the Middle East escalation, we need to report Iran's attacks on the Ras Laffan in Qatar. This strike had removed 13 million tonnes of Qatar's LNG export capacity. Looking at the press release, they communicated that they will "declare force majeure on LNG contracts for up to five years." Therefore, it would take years to repair the damage.

There are two considerations for Golar:

  1. part of the company's upside is linked to gas price evolution, and this is positive for Hilli and MKIl, and
  2. this negative event is likely to raise Golar awareness and FLNG offering.

This is because the company produces floating liquefied natural gas vessels with a shorter time-to-market (3-4 years). Additionally, the company communicated cost inflation for equipment (especially gas turbines); however, Golar maintains a cost advantage over onshore alternatives of approximately 40%.

For the first time, the company reported "increasing demand for incremental FLNG project developments" (Fig. 2). So there is a valuation for additional FLNG units. Given that each unit generates more than $200 million in EBITDA per year, this could have significant upside for Golar's valuation.

Fig 2

Lastly, the company reported a Q4 adjusted EBITDA of $91 million, above the Wall Street estimate of $85.6 million. Higher top-line sales drove this beat, along with lower-than-expected G&A. Golar maintained its quarterly cash dividend and repurchased and canceled $43 million of common shares. More importantly, the company completed a refinancing for the Gimi facility for a total of $1.2 billion. We report positively on Golar's debt management activities, and, more importantly, Golar communicated that "we think that it's almost better to buy back our own stock at an undervaluation than to do anything else effectively." This is very supportive.

Valuation

It is very hard to increase our EBITDA estimates on higher gas price curves. In a supportive scenario, we see Golar EBITDA up by $45-$50 million per year. However, this isn't part of our playbook. We continue to value the company with a long-term contracted EBITDA of $850 million. So, we derive an enterprise value of $8.5 billion at a 10x EV/EBITDA multiple (unchanged from our last analysis). The company's debt totaled $2.7 billion, but after accounting for cash, the net debt was $1.5 billion. Compared to our last analysis, Golar slightly increased its net debt (we estimate it at $1.31 billion). This was due to the ongoing buyback. In our equity bridge, we also include $500 million in capex awarded for the required infrastructure for the FLNG Hilli and MKII FLNG projects. Therefore, total cash outflow is estimated at $2 billion, moving Golar's equity value to $6.5 billion. Considering a current market cap of $5.5 billion, there is an 18% upside, and with the current dividend payment, Golar's 1-year total return could reached 20%. So we remain buyers with a target price of $65 per share.

Risks

Golar risks include higher CAPEX, which could be detrimental to the equity value bridge. As reported in our analysis, we see CAPEX inflation risks, especially for turbines. Golar is subject to clients' concentration and operates in EM; however, the company operates under English Law, with dispute resolution pursuant to International Chamber of Commerce arbitration in France. The company may be exposed to technological changes, and, given its current debt, Golar is sensitive to higher interest rates.

Conclusion

All in all, we see upside ahead in the Golar investment equity story. We positively report the Goldman Sachs appointment for strategic optionality to maximize value. Additionally, the ongoing conflicts and energy diversification require an infrastructure player such as Golar.

data.title: Enovix: Simply Not Good Enough
data.description:
The bear case for Enovix (ENVX) is simple: the company has not gotten it done. The optimism around the company's 2021 SPAC (special purpose acquisition company) merger faded when the company's production equipment basically failed to perform as hoped. ENVX stock plunged 41% after the Q3 2022 earnings release when that problem was disclosed.

The arrival of T.J. Rodgers as executive chairman just days later sparked turnaround hopes, given Rodgers's success with Cypress Semiconductor and then Enphase Energy (ENPH). Rodgers quickly installed a new chief executive officer, Raj Talluri, with the clear goal of improving execution and getting Enovix's innovative silicon-anode batteries into production.

That hasn't worked, either. Deadlines continue to slip, while Talluri has (by his own admission at a fireside chat in December) overpromised and underdelivered. Rodgers has made, shall we say, unsubstantiated allegations against short sellers, while Enovix's capital allocation (alongside Rodgers's own comments) has seemed designed at least in part to play up the "short squeeze" angle and boost the stock. Here, too, the strategy hasn't succeeded: ENVX hit an all-time low last month and short interest remains significantly elevated.

The bull case, meanwhile, is that these short-term failures are simply delays, rather than a sign that the long-term case for Enovix stock is failing. To be sure, Enovix is undertaking an exceptionally difficult effort. Silicon-anode batteries have never been commercialized. And the opportunity obviously is massive: Enovix originally targeted the smartphone market, but management (particularly of late) has pointed to additional opportunities in smart eyewear, drones, and defense. If the company can finally develop a battery that offers (as management has detailed), longer life, better performance, and faster charging, the potential market is enormous, particularly with generative artificial intelligence driving ever-greater demands on battery-powered devices.

The problem, however, is that 'if' is just too big. With yet another disappointment delivered in Q4, it's become nearly impossible to have much faith in the company. And even if Enovix can drive a turnaround, the potential upside here remains significant enough that it seems wiser to buy after the company shows some progress rather than before. Without any progress — and Q4 showed little to none — Enovix's history still casts too large a shadow over its future.

The Honor Problem

In September 2024, Enovix entered into a development agreement with Chinese smartphone manufacturer Honor. Over the following months, the two companies worked together to qualify Enovix batteries for Honor products.

On the Q3 call, Talluri noted that while Enovix had come close to meeting Honor's requirements, it hadn't quite gotten there. The core stumbling point seemed to be over a 1,000 charge/discharge cycle test; as Talluri put it on the call, Enovix products could get close to that number, but near the end of the test performance would start to slip. And so Enovix agreed to a "design iteration", apparently focused on the actual chemistry of the batteries, which in turn would meet Honor's requirements. As Talluri put it in the Q&A of the call:

We realized that we need to make one more small design change to get to the full performance that they want. And we validated that change now internally, and we have confidence that, that change we made will actually go to the 1,000 cycles requirement.

We're now making batteries to that specification, and we expect to get those batteries and send them out to Honor this quarter, and they will start the testing again. And as you know, batteries are one of those things where when you give a new design iteration, we got to start the cycle life testing again, and it takes 3 to 4 months to do it because of just the nature of the process. We expect that to get done in 1Q next year, and then we expect to get the commercialization after that.

That's not what happened, however. As of the Q1 call in late February, Honor still hadn't qualified the product, and in fact Enovix seems to have made basically zero progress in the nearly four months between earnings releases. The answer from Talluri now in fact seems to be that Enovix can't meet Honor's existing requirements, given the three scenarios to qualification he laid out after Q1:

  1. Honor basically accepts testing under different conditions, notably around how much of the battery is discharged (0.2C versus 0.7C, or discharging the battery fully in five hours instead of 1.4 hours);
  2. A new "accelerated testing protocol" suitable for silicon-anode batteries. Talluri has argued (likely with some justification) that current testing structure doesn't reflect real-world usage and that the new type of battery perhaps merit a new test;
  3. Continued chemistry changes to meet the existing 0.7C test that Enovix hasn't been able to completely pass to this point.

The fact that a) the 0.7C test apparently has been a stumbling block for potentially a year (ie, a few months after Honor came on as lead customer ~18 months ago) and b) Talluri is highlighting the other two options first both suggest the third option is unlikely to arrive soon, if at all. (If the CEO was confident that chemistry changes would let batteries pass the 0.7C test, he wouldn't have spent months trying to get Honor to junk that very same test.)

But the very fact that Honor hasn't agreed to either of the other two scenarios itself is a problem (and one that grows bigger by the day). These are essentially binary decisions from Honor: either the smartphone manufacturer accepts Enovix's arguments about altering testing specifications or it doesn't. This doesn't seem like a negotiable point, particularly because Enovix has no leverage. It can't threaten to withhold a product that doesn't actually yet exist (at least in commercialized form). Meanwhile, Honor would be taking a very real risk (from its perspective) in using Enovix batteries: shipping a few million phones that were potentially found to have substandard batteries would create massive costs and damage to the brand.

And so investors have to at least contemplate the potential that the partnership here simply is going to end. If Enovix can't get over the finish line at some point Honor is going to move on. That point is absolutely destructive for the bull case here. Enovix ostensibly chose Honor as lead customer in the first place because it was the manufacturer most willing to work with it and consider a form of battery that, again, has never been commercialized before. If Honor walks away, it's exceptionally difficult to see what options Enovix has. There aren't that many smartphone manufacturers, Enovix has had years to attempt to reach out to them, and if any others were more enthused than Honor in 2024 they would themselves be the lead customer(s).

So the issues with Honor are not just a setback, or a delay. They legitimately appear to threaten the bull case for ENVX stock that has existed over the nearly five years the company has been public.

Can Enovix Even Commercialize Its Batteries?

Again, it's possible that analysis is too pessimistic, and that Enovix does find a way to reach qualification with Honor in the not-too-distant future. No doubt, that would drive a huge upside move for ENVX stock; from a short-term perspective, in fact, the stock is likely a bet on whether Honor qualification happens or not. (From here, that's a reason to stay on the sidelines at the least, but some readers admittedly may view it differently.)

But taking the longer-view, the last few years and quarters raise concerns about an issue that doesn't get as much coverage, which is whether Enovix can actually make any money off these batteries. Qualification at a single customer might drive a big pop in the stock, but for Enovix as a business it's only one step, and an early step at that.

At the very least, Enovix hasn't proven that it can do so. On the Q4 call, the issue of laser dicing in the manufacturing process came up again as a potential "limiting factor" in throughput. Yields are nearing 80% overall, but the dicing problems are driving lower performance (though, to be fair, Talluri said the performance has improved in the first two months of this year).

Overall, Talluri continues to argue that any yield and throughput concerns will be resolved — but that in and of itself highlights a core problem with the bull case, which is that it's at this point impossible to take that at face value. Talluri was saying months after taking over that full-scale manufacturing would begin in late 2024 and ramp across 2025. Commercialization per the Q4 call is now "late '26 or beginning in 2027", and even that's far from guaranteed.

But importantly, that's not the final step, or anywhere close. In fact, it's actually the less difficult step — and Enovix hasn't even started taking it yet. It's been commercialization and yields where battery upstarts have often stumbled, and Enovix hasn't proved it can tackle either problem yet.

No, ENVX Stock Is Not Cheap

One retort to these issues is that Enovix actually can still prosper with a small (or perhaps even nonexistent) smartphone business, because other attractive end markets now are available. Talluri made this case after Q4, and so did the company's earnings presentation:

And there is some logic to Talluri's point, which is that smart eyewear and drones are actually easier markets to get qualified in. Drones, for instance, have plenty of space if a battery needs to expand (the core issue for silicon-anode, and one which Enovix plans to solve through the structure of the casing as much as the interior chemistry). Smart eyewear customer have lower bars to clear for qualification; the discussion around the back-and-forth with Honor does suggest that Enovix might have little issue getting qualified for eyewear.

But even though the slide above suggests relatively large addressable markets, it's not at all clear that they are big enough to support the current ENVX stock price. The company still has an enterprise value of over $1 billion. Margins in the industry are lower, with gross margins often in the teens and operating margins for most consumer players in the low to mid-single-digits:

Ostensibly, Enovix would garner higher margins with a product that worked in the way management believes it will. Even so, a $1B-plus EV still prices in at least a couple billion dollars in annual revenue at some point several years out. (Just back of the envelope, something like $2 billion in 2032 sales at a 10% operating margin probably supports the current EV, given there is obviously a very real risk of a zero here if the technology simply can't be produced at scale.)

But per the call, the total addressable market for smart eyewear is supposed to be $400 million by 2030. Per the slide above, defense and drones is ~$3 billion now; that's maybe $5 billion down the line (right now, drones are about half the figure).

In other words, in a scenario where smartphone revenue doesn't materialize, Enovix stock still is pricing in not just a couple wins in eyewear and drones, but material market share in a hugely competitive market. That's with minimal business development efforts or infrastructure in those industries at the moment. The risks there in practice strongly suggest that "Enovix can just pivot away from smartphones" is not a solid enough bull case to own shares here.

So even at the lows, Enovix stock still is pricing in some success here. And to return to the point made at the beginning of this article, from a high level to this point Enovix basically has had zero success. (The Routejade acquisition admittedly does seem like a smart move, but in the context of valuation it's just not big enough or profitable enough to materially change the investment case.)

And even in a scenario where Enovix does find a way to commercialize smartphone batteries, there is enough upside that trying to time the win doesn't seem wise from a risk/reward standpoint. ENVX might well go to $20-plus on the back of a legitimate, consistent, profitable smartphone business. (That hypothetical valuation might well be conservative; the stock traded at $20-plus quite often over the past few years, suggesting a path toward much higher levels if the blue-sky scenario played out.). Owning it at $9 with some evidence of success and a clearer path toward billions in revenue seems wiser from a risk/reward standpoint than trying to time the bottom at $5.

After all, the bull case here is triple-digit upside, and the bear case is zero. It would seem to make more sense betting on the bull case when and if Enovix finally provides a concrete reason to do so.

data.title: SCHP: Schwab TIPS Fund Provides A Good Medium Duration Alternative To VTIP
data.description:
TIPS are Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities issued by the US Government. This type of security is described further in the Summary Prospectus of Schwab U.S. TIPS ETF (SCHP), which is also the fund that we will be covering today.

TIPS are publicly issued, dollar denominated U.S. Government securities issued by the U.S. Treasury that have principal and interest payments linked to an official inflation measure (as measured by the Consumer Price Index, or CPI) and their payments are supported by the full faith and credit of the United States.

Source: Summary Prospectus

SCHP is a passive exchange-traded fund that pursues the performance of the Bloomberg US Treasury Inflation-Linked Bond Index (Series-L). The index does not have any expenses; however, the fund does.

The expenses are nominal but should still be taken into account when comparing the performance of the ETF versus its benchmark index. We can see that SCHP has comfortably achieved its investment objective, which is "to track as closely as possible, before fees and expenses, the total return of an index composed of inflation-protected U.S. Treasury securities."

The fund also sports 4 stars (out of 5) for overall performance from Morningstar.

We look at whether this would make a good holding for your portfolio today.

Why TIPS?

With nominal yield bonds, you are locking in the duration risk and the inflation risk. If inflation turns out to be higher than expected over the course of the bond term, your real returns will be lower. TIPS are a fix for the second problem. With TIPS, you only lock in a "real" return over the duration of the bond. This means you are taking some duration risk (depending on the length of the bond), but you are not messing with inflation risk. If inflation (as measured by the Consumer Price Index, or CPI) soars over the period, well, you will make a lot more dough. Perhaps the best visualization of this can be done via the post-COVID-19 period. The government had unleashed the largest fiscal stimulus of all time, and the Fed had also stepped up to the stupidity by buying inflated mortgage-backed securities. Inflation soared. You can see how SCHP outperformed a standard iShares 7-10 Treasury Bond ETF (IEF) during this time.

Yes, it still had negative returns, but that positive differential comes entirely from the surprise in inflation.

One reason that SCHP did not do even better is because the real rates soared during that timeframe. Investors who were happy to purchase bonds at negative real rates (guaranteed loss after inflation), simply because they read somewhere that bond duration should match their expected life span, found out the hard way.

The gains from extra inflation payments were offset partially by this soaring real rates effect on the portfolio. That brings us to the key attraction today. The real rate, as shown above, while not extremely high, is at least decent. You are locking in a modest real rate, and that means your odds of making money over 5 years are extremely high.

Verdict on SCHP

While we have not covered SCHP prior to this, we have written on Vanguard Short-Term Inflation-Protected Sec Index ETF (VTIP) several times. Even during our maximum bearish phase on bonds, we found this one appealing. The key difference between VTIP and SCHP comes down to duration. VTIP has a very short effective duration of 2.5 years, and that makes it virtually risk-free as an investment. You are taking no credit risk, and you are taking no inflation risk. What you are taking with VTIP is just mild duration risk. In fact, during the post-COVID-19 blowout in bonds, VTIP actually managed to even clobber SCHP.

But today, we think that SCHP's extra duration is not a bad idea. About half of its portfolio has a duration that averages similar to VTIP (the 1-3 years and 3-5 years categories). The rest is the extra duration that we are talking about.

You want to lock in that real rate, at least on a portion of your investments, and we think SCHP does a reasonably good job at that. While bonds remain hated, we think this is a good time to add some inflation-protected bonds to the mix. The main risk (we can't use "real risk" here) is that real rates soar. We don't believe that outcome will be allowed, as that would rapidly lead to a US Treasury default. But in all normal circumstances, these bonds should provide a good diversification for your portfolio. While we do not own these, we do own some real return bonds in Canada, which function on the same principle.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, and sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Trade Desk: Macro Headwinds Aside, This Is A Tremendous Bargain (Upgrade)
data.description:
There's no doubt that 2026 has introduced substantial volatility into both the stock market and the "real economy." Overall, companies are watching a trend of potentially weaker consumer spending, which has resulted in many brands pulling back on advertising spend.

The weaker advertising climate, plus a rising narrative that Amazon (AMZN) and AI are growing threats, has doused cold water on The Trade Desk, Inc. (TTD), formerly a market darling that has now lost ~85% of its peak value and is down ~50% since the start of the year alone. The question for investors now is, when does the bleeding stop?

I last wrote a "Buy" article on The Trade Desk in December, when the stock was trading at $39 per share. Needless to say, I didn't predict the SaaSpocalypse or for continued pessimism around The Trade Desk to tank the share price, and my earlier "Buy" call was ill-timed. That said, when I take a fresh look at the company's fundamentals now (including and especially its decimated valuation multiples), I find a deeply oversold stock that may require patience but is ripe for a turnaround. I'm upgrading my viewpoint on The Trade Desk to a "Strong Buy."

Deep Valuation Discount Ahead Of Solid Guidance For FY26

A "Strong Buy" rating represents a high level of conviction that I have in very few stocks, and in The Trade Desk's case, my high degree of conviction lies primarily in the fact that there is a huge valuation safety net underneath this stock. Herd mentality has intensified in the stock market of 2026, which has driven many investors to flee to safety, especially after "SaaSpocalypse" fears. But we shouldn't ignore the stocks that have crashed and offer deep value.

At current share prices under $20, The Trade Desk trades at a market cap of $9.57 billion. We note the company's latest balance sheet has $1.30 billion of cash, completely unencumbered by debt. Adjusting for this gives us an enterprise value of $8.27 billion.

The Trade Desk only guides out one quarter at a time, but we can utilize the company's latest expectations to construct a viewpoint on the company's potential for profitability this year. For Q1, the company has guided to $678 million in revenue (+10% y/y) and $195 million in adjusted EBITDA, or a 29% margin that represents ~5 points of downside versus a 34% margin in FY25.

Is the slowdown to ~10% growth, plus the expectation of a pullback in adjusted EBITDA margins, concerning? Absolutely: We won't completely ignore the fact that The Trade Desk sits in a far more competitive advertising landscape, and the fact that the company is expecting margins to flatten is a signal of the intensifying competitive landscape that investors have been fearing. But at the same time, these risks are priced in.

For the full year FY26, Wall Street is expecting The Trade Desk to generate $3.26 billion in revenue, or 13% y/y growth. If we assume that full-year margins follow the same seasonality as in FY25 and decline 5 points (as suggested by the company's Q1 guidance) to a 36% full-year margin (versus 41% for the full year FY25), then FY26 adjusted EBITDA would be $1.17 billion (-2% y/y). Also, Street estimates are pointing to $2.06 in pro forma EPS (+16% y/y), driven in large part by declining share counts.

This positions The Trade Desk's valuation at:

  • 7.1x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA.
  • 9.7x FY26 P/E.
  • 8.4x FY26 ex-cash P/E.

Again, we emphasize that the low multiples help to compensate for a riskier operating landscape. At the same time, the market is treating The Trade Desk like a company that is at near-term risk of implosion, when in reality, it's still a business that is generating strong earnings. We suspect that these multiples, at the very least, attract potential acquirers (whether PE firms looking for healthy cash flow in a completely unlevered company or a potential strategic acquirer like Alphabet (GOOG), who could combine The Trade Desk's automated ad-buying software with its reams of digital inventory).

Q4 Wasn't As Bad As Feared

The other point to make is that, despite the negativity that has built up in this stock, its actual fundamental reality and execution aren't disastrous. Take a look at the Q4 earnings summary below:

The Trade Desk's revenue grew 14% y/y to $846.8 million, which actually still beat Wall Street's expectations of $840.7 million (+13% y/y) by a one-point margin. Recall as well that the company had a tough prior-year comp against the 2024 election cycle last year. Management notes that excluding revenue tied to political advertising, Q4 growth would have clocked in at 19% y/y (decelerating three points from 22% growth in Q3, adjusted for political advertising in that quarter as well).

There are two main concerns that investors have about The Trade Desk right now: its sales performance, which is being colored by the macroeconomy, and the rise of Amazon's DSP as a threat to The Trade Desk. Though we certainly don't favor The Trade Desk's revenue deceleration (especially to ~10% growth in Q1), we also have to keep the macro in mind. Per CEO Jeffrey Green's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

2025 was fantastic for tech spend, for travel spend, for pharma spend and for communication spend. Actually, despite a greater degree of macro uncertainty and most S&P 500 companies trying to determine what changes the evolving AI-fueled world and the geopolitical issues mean for them, most categories had a very good year. One of the clearest themes in our data and from our conversations with clients was a sustained weakness among some large consumer packaged goods companies, CPGs as well as some global auto companies.

Together, these verticals represent over 1/4 of our business. But in these 2 categories, all global companies have levels of uncertainty that we haven't seen for most of the last 15 years. They all had tough choices to make in 2025. Most still have tough choices ahead. I do want to be clear that some autos and CPGs have done very well in this environment, especially those that have focused on growth, value, objectivity and impact.

But some have had to respond to the impact of those macro pressures and instead just focus on reducing costs. In other cases, some companies have shrunk branding spend and focused on cost cutting instead of focusing on growing. When these companies are excluded from our year-over-year comparison, our business in the open internet is doing much better than the averages alone would suggest.

We think these are transitory macro headwinds that we shouldn't overly punish The Trade Desk for. The U.S. economy is shaky but more robust than many thought (as evidenced by the strong recent jobs report). The Trade Desk should benefit once businesses pivot back to growth mode, which may happen upon greater clarity on the end of the Iran conflict and an accommodating Fed.

The other issue is Amazon. Yes, it's true that Amazon has made growth in advertising revenue a major priority. At the same time, The Trade Desk's position is that Amazon is primarily concerned with selling inventory on Amazon (on Prime Video and in the marketplace). Yes, ad buyers on Amazon have better access to Amazon's retail data, and Amazon has tremendous pricing power to cut its platform fees (potentially to zero).

But businesses typically want to advertise across a wide variety of places in what The Trade Desk calls "the open internet," and that's where The Trade Desk fills buyer demand that can't be handled by Amazon. With The Trade Desk still projecting to grow double digits in FY26 despite a weak macro, we find that it's difficult to make the argument that Amazon is totally killing off The Trade Desk.

In Q4, The Trade Desk grew adjusted EBITDA 14% y/y to $400.3 million, at a 47% margin that held flat y/y. Full-year adjusted EBITDA of $1.20 billion also grew 18% y/y, with margins holding flat at 41%.

The company's Q1 guidance, which calls for margins eroding 5 points y/y, is the first time the company will have seen margins decline. This may be a conservative outlook that The Trade Desk may end up overcoming.

Risks And Key Takeaways

The next major catalyst for The Trade Desk will be its Q1 earnings report, due in mid/late May. Of course, there are risks here that we need to keep in mind. The macro situation likely worsened considerably relative to when the company issued its Q4 earnings (in February), thanks to the run-up in oil prices. It may take time for The Trade Desk to benefit from the macro stabilization that will help to resuscitate its revenue.

In my view, however, the stock's single-digit P/E and EBITDA multiples more than adequately price in macro and Amazon competitive risks. Use the dip as a buying opportunity.
